personality_voice.book_baseline: Frank is established as stubborn, quietly courageous, and defiant under pressure. He is a war veteran with a deep distrust of authority (especially police), a loner who endures community hostility without yielding, and a man who acts on his own judgment. His voice in the confrontation scene is direct, rough, and combative: 'I don't think much of your manners, My Lord. Turn round and face me like a man, why don't you?' He bluffs ('my wife knows I'm up here'), shows fear physically (sweating, trembling hands) but does not break verbally. He is not passive - he processes what he hears, forms a plan, and chooses to stay and listen rather than flee. His internal monologue shows a man reasoning under terror.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: Frank appears only in brief, mostly silent action: he sees the light, enters the house, overhears voices, is spotted by Nagini, and is killed. His only spoken line in the film is 'Bloody kids' - directed at the Weasley twins offscreen, not at Voldemort. The defiant confrontation with Voldemort is entirely absent. He is killed without speaking a word to his killer.
personality_voice.difference: The film removes Frank's entire verbal confrontation with Voldemort - the scene that most directly establishes his personality. His defiance, his bluffing, his rough directness, his war-veteran courage under pressure, and his stubborn refusal to be intimidated are all absent. 'Bloody kids' is a throwaway line that establishes only that he is a grumpy old man. The film retains his physical action (going to investigate) but strips the voice and personality that define him.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The confrontation scene is not incidental - it is the only scene where Frank's character is directly expressed through dialogue. Without it, the film shows a man who walks into a house and is killed silently. The core traits (defiance, rough courage, verbal stubbornness) are not preserved. The character remains recognizable as 'old man who investigates and dies' but the defining personality traits are removed. Score falls in the 14-17 range: recognizable but important traits are reduced.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Frank's narrative role is dual: (1) he is the vehicle through which the reader witnesses the Voldemort/Wormtail scene and learns critical plot information, and (2) he is a humanizing victim whose death gives moral weight to Voldemort's return. His agency within the scene is active: he decides to investigate, he chooses to stay and listen rather than flee, he processes the information and forms a plan to go to the police, he confronts Voldemort directly and verbally challenges him. He is not merely a passive observer - he makes decisions throughout.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Frank retains his structural function as the POV witness to the Voldemort scene and as the victim whose death opens the film. However, his agency within the scene is almost entirely removed. He does not confront Voldemort, does not speak to him, does not challenge him, does not form a plan. He is spotted by Nagini and killed immediately. He is a passive observer who becomes a passive victim.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film preserves the structural role (witness/victim) but removes the active agency. In the books, Frank decides to stay, decides to confront, chooses to bluff, chooses to challenge. In the film, he simply walks in and is killed. The decision-making and confrontation that constitute his agency are absent.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer rule applies partially: Frank's agency (confronting Voldemort, speaking defiantly) is not transferred to another character - it is simply removed. This is an agency loss. The structural role as witness/victim is preserved, which prevents a lower score, but the active dimension of his role is gone. Score falls in the 10-13 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Frank's motivations are layered: (1) civic duty and suspicion of vandalism drive him to investigate; (2) distrust of police means he acts alone rather than calling authorities; (3) once inside, he stays because he recognizes criminal activity and feels compelled to gather information; (4) he is torn between the impulse to flee to the police and the compulsion to keep listening. His internal conflict - wanting to leave but being unable to - is explicitly narrated: 'He wanted more than anything to be back in his bed with his hot-water bottle. The problem was that his legs didn't seem to want to move.'
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film shows Frank investigating (motivation: sees a light, assumes intruders) but does not show any internal conflict. There is no moment where he considers leaving, no moment where he processes what he hears and decides to stay. The motivation to investigate is preserved; the internal conflict between fear and compulsion is absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The primary motivation (investigate the light) is preserved. The secondary motivations (distrust of police, civic duty) are implied by his solo action. The internal conflict - the paralysis between fleeing and staying, the moral weight of what he overhears - is entirely absent from the film.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The core motivation is present but the internal conflict is removed. This is a partial alteration - the motivation is simplified to 'old man investigates light' without the layered reasoning and paralysis that the books establish. Score falls in the 8-10 range: motivations partially present but flattened.

character_arc.book_baseline: Frank's arc within GoF is compact but complete: he begins as a man unjustly suspected of murder who has endured decades of community hostility with stubborn dignity; he ends as a man who, in his final moments, demonstrates the same courage and defiance that defined his wartime self. The arc is not one of change but of revelation - the final scene confirms who he has always been. Dumbledore's eulogy at the end of the book ('he has not been seen since last August') frames him as a forgotten victim, adding a final note of injustice.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the beginning (isolated old man, unjustly suspected) and the end (killed by Voldemort). The revelation arc - the confirmation of his character through his final confrontation - is absent because the confrontation is absent. He dies without the moment that would have confirmed his courage.
character_arc.difference: The arc's endpoint is structurally present (he dies) but the meaning of that endpoint is changed. In the books, he dies having faced Voldemort defiantly. In the film, he dies having said nothing. The arc is compressed to the point where the defining moment is lost.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but the structural shape (suspected man dies as forgotten victim) is present. However, the key turning point - the confrontation that reveals his character - is missing. This is an incomplete arc rather than a false one. Score falls in the 11-13 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Frank's key relationship in the corpus is his adversarial confrontation with Voldemort. This is not a relationship in the ongoing sense, but it is the defining dynamic of his appearance: Frank as the defiant, morally clear ordinary man facing an incomprehensible evil. The power imbalance is absolute, but Frank refuses to be diminished by it. His relationship with the village (community hostility, his stubborn refusal to leave) is also established, though no individual relationship is developed.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film shows Frank in proximity to Voldemort but without any exchange. Voldemort says 'Nagini tells me the old Muggle caretaker is standing just outside the door' and 'Avada Kedavra' - Frank never responds. The dynamic of defiance against power is entirely absent. The village relationship is not shown at all.
key_relationships.difference: The confrontation dynamic - the only relationship the corpus develops - is removed. Frank does not speak to Voldemort, does not challenge him, does not register as anything other than an obstacle to be eliminated. The emotional and dramatic function of the relationship (ordinary human dignity vs. inhuman evil) is lost.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The relationship's dramatic function is changed: in the books it reveals Frank's character and gives moral weight to Voldemort's cruelty; in the film it is a one-sided execution with no exchange. The dynamic is not preserved. Score falls in the 5-6 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Frank's complexity comes from several sources: (1) the injustice of his situation - a decent man destroyed by community suspicion; (2) his stubborn dignity in the face of that injustice; (3) his war-veteran psychology (courage under fire, distrust of authority, physical limitation); (4) his internal reasoning process as he listens to Voldemort; (5) his defiant verbal confrontation; (6) the pathos of his death as a forgotten Muggle victim. He is a minor character but a fully realized one - his complexity is concentrated in a single chapter.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film retains: isolated old man, investigates light, is killed. It removes: the community backstory, the war veteran psychology, the internal reasoning, the defiant confrontation, the verbal challenge to Voldemort, and Dumbledore's eulogy. 'Bloody kids' is the only line that characterizes him, and it characterizes him only as a grumpy old man.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The majority of what makes Frank a complex minor character is removed. What remains is a functional placeholder: old man who witnesses the scene and dies. The nuance, the backstory, the defiance, the pathos of his specific situation - all absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is a major flattening. The film reduces Frank to a single function (witness/victim) and a single trait (grumpy old man). The complexity that makes him memorable in the books - his dignity, his courage, his unjust history - is almost entirely absent. Score falls in the 5-7 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Frank's verbal confrontation with Voldemort ('Turn round and face me like a man, why don't you?') - removed entirely
Frank's defiant bluff about his wife knowing where he is - removed
Frank's internal reasoning process while listening to Voldemort's conversation - removed
Frank's decision to stay and listen rather than flee - removed (shown only as passive observation)
Frank's war-veteran psychology: courage under fire, distrust of police, physical limitation as character texture - removed
The community backstory: decades of unjust suspicion, stubborn refusal to leave Little Hangleton - removed
Frank's physical fear responses (sweating, trembling) contrasted with verbal defiance - removed
Dumbledore's end-of-book eulogy framing Frank as a forgotten Muggle victim - removed (not in film corpus)
Frank's recognition that Voldemort is a murderer and his moral decision to act - removed
score_caps_applied: Cap 4 (score above 70 not applicable): the film preserves only Frank's broad identity - old man who investigates and dies - while removing most of what defines his book characterization
Cap 3 (score above 75 not applicable): Frank's defining act of agency - the verbal confrontation with Voldemort - is removed entirely
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Frank Bryce's structural function (POV witness to the Voldemort scene, first victim of the restored Voldemort) and his broad identity (isolated old man, caretaker of the Riddle House) but removes almost everything that makes him a character rather than a plot device. The single most important scene in his book portrayal - his defiant verbal confrontation with Voldemort - is entirely absent. Without it, his defining traits (rough courage, stubborn dignity, war-veteran defiance) are invisible. The film reduces him to a silent figure who walks into a room and is killed, characterized only by 'Bloody kids.' The injustice of his situation, his internal reasoning, his community backstory, and the pathos of his death as a forgotten Muggle are all lost. He is recognizable as the same person in the same situation, but the characterization that makes that situation meaningful is gone.
